content.full_text:
Memorandu 
To 
: Members of the Commission 
cate 
March 30, 1978 
From 
I 
F AIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ted Prim 
/ 
Subject: Sources of Income to Brokers 
Recently, the staff had a question involving the source 
of income to an insurance broker. 
The following background 
was provided to me by representatives of the Department of 
Insurance. 
Insurance "agents" contract with one or more insurance 
companies to sell insurance to the public. 
Thus, an agent 
may represent, for example, both New York Life and Prudential 
Insurance. 
When the agent sells a policy to a customer, the 
agent either forwards the premium payment to the insurance 
company or holds the money in trust for the company. 
At some 
agreed upon point in time, the insurance company either pays 
the agent or authorizes him to permanent Iv withhold some of 
the funds as his commission. 
An insurance "broker" technically represents the cus-
tomer and not an insurance company. 
Thus, t-lhen the customer 
comes to a broker and asks to purchase insurance, the broker 
will help the customer find appropriate insurance. 
However, 
it is a fact that most brokers also are insurance agents. 
Therefore, in assisting the customer to find appropriate 
insurance, the broker usually attempts to steer the customer 
in the direction of the companies for which the broker is 
an agent. 
Some insurance agents/brokers employ persons known as 
"solicitors". 
They actually work for a broker or agent, 
usually on a commission basis. 
Thus, they are analogous 
to the real estate salesman who works for a real estate 
broker. 
However, unlike the real estate situation, sales-
men are not required to affiliate themselves with a broker. 
Therefore, it is my understanding that solicitors comprise 
a much smaller portion of the insurance industry than do 
salesmen in the real estate industry. 


Members of the Commission 
March 30, 1978 
Page Two 
In the real estate field, we have consistently advised 
that the source of income to the broker is the seller of 
property.lI This is the case regardless of whether the broker 
is representing the seller or buyer in the transaction. 
In 
the Carey opinion, we concluded that the "income" to the 
broker did not include the commission which he was required 
to pay his salesmen. 
In the case of the insurance agent/broker, there is an 
interest in having the names of both the insurance company 
and the buyer defined as sources of income. 
Obviously, an 
agent who represents one or more insurance companies may be 
inclined to protect the interests of those companies. 
More-
over, the agent physically receives his payments from the 
insurance company. 
However, the agent/broker also attempts 
to build and cultivate relationships with his regular clients. 
One can easily conceive of situations in which a city council-
man who was both an insurance agent/broker would be biased in 
favor of both "sources". 
Examples: 
1) 
The City Council may be preparing to 
award a contract to an insurance company to provide insurance 
to city employees. 
If a member of the council is the agent 
for the company in question, should he be permitted to make 
or participate in the making of this decision? 
2) 
ABC Construction Co. has purchased its insurance 
through Councilman X for the last 10 years. 
ABC is now 
before the Council regarding a proposed development. 
Should 
Councilman X be permitted to make or participate in the making 
of this decision? 
Both for purposes of disclosure and disqualification, 
who is the source of income to an insurance agent/broker? 
The company or the buyer, or both? 
Similar questions arise with respect to the appropriate 
source of income for real estate agents -
the broker, the buyer 
or the seller. 
The broker seems to be the most logical person 
!/ This assumes that the broker works on commission as 
a partner or sole proprietor of a real estate brokerage firm. 


Members of the Commission 
March 30, 1978 
Page Three 
to report as the source since the agent works for him albeit 
on a commission basis. 
There is an inconsistency, however, in 
stating that the broker is the source of income to the agent 
since the Carey opinion concludes that the broker is merely a 
conduit for funds paid by the seller to the agent. 
Thus, the 
broker does not include as part of his income the commissions 
which he is obligated to pay to his agents. 
It, therefore, 
would be somewhat anomalous to conclude that the agent should 
report these funds as having been received from the broker. 
If we conclude that the broker is not the source of income to 
the agent, we would be permitting the agent to make governmen-
tal decisions which foreseeably and materially affect the 
broker, who is in effect his employer. 
To conclude that the- source of incom~ to the agent is always 
the seller technically may be accurate but is unsatisfying in 
several respects. 
First, as mentioned above, the agent really 
works for the broker. 
Second, the agent may have been chosen 
by the buyer to find him a piece of property. 
Thus, the work-
ing relationship is with the buyer rather than the seller. 
In view of these considerations, the Commission might wish 
to conclude that real estate agents have a dual source of 
income -- the broker and either the buyer or the seller, 
depending upon which one is the agent's client. 
TP:mfa

sections.question_synthetic: What should be identified as the source of income for insurance agents/brokers and real estate agents for purposes of FPPC disclosure and disqualification rules, and how should conflicts of interest be addressed when these professionals serve in government positions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff suggests that real estate agents may have dual sources of income (the broker and either the buyer or seller depending on who is the client), and raises unresolved questions about whether insurance agents/brokers should report income from insurance companies, customers, or both, given the potential conflicts of interest in each scenario.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal staff memorandum to Commission members rather than a formal advice letter response to an outside inquiry. It presents background information and raises questions for Commission consideration without providing a definitive conclusion. No formal Question/Conclusion sections are present; the document is analytical and exploratory in nature.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What should be identified as the source of income for insurance agents/brokers and real estate agents for purposes of FPPC disclosure and disqualification rules, and how should conflicts of interest be addressed when these professionals serve in government positions?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC staff suggests that real estate agents may have dual sources of income (the broker and either the buyer or seller depending on who is the client), and raises unresolved questions about whether insurance agents/brokers should report income from insurance companies, customers, or both, given the potential conflicts of interest in each scenario.

embedding.summary: This internal FPPC memorandum from Ted Prim to Commission members analyzes the sources of income for insurance brokers and real estate agents, discussing potential conflicts of interest when these professionals hold government positions and proposing that agents may have multiple income sources depending on their contractual relationships.
